back: A vector space V over field F (R or C) with an inner product <u,v> that maps pairs of vectors to F, satisfying: conjugate symmetry, linearity in first argument, and positive-definiteness.

front: What are the axioms of inner product?
Define the norm of a vector in an inner product space
back: The norm (length) of vector v is ||v|| = √(<v,v>). It represents the distance from origin to point v.
For any u,v in inner product space: |<u,v>| ≤ ||u|| ||v||. Equality holds iff u and v are linearly dependent.
For any u,v: ||u+v|| ≤ ||u|| + ||v||. The norm of sum is at most sum of norms.
Two vectors u and v are orthogonal if <u,v> = 0. Denoted as u ⟂ v. Zero vector is orthogonal to all vectors.
A set of vectors where each pair is orthogonal (<ui,uj> = 0 for i≠j) and each vector has unit norm (||ui|| = 1).
A vector v is a unit vector if ||v|| = 1. Any non-zero vector v can be normalized to u = v/||v||.
A method to convert any set of linearly independent vectors into an orthogonal/orthonormal set. For each vector, subtract its projections onto previous orthogonal vectors.

front: Define the adjoint (Hermitian transpose) of a linear operator
back: For operator T: V→V, the adjoint T* satisfies <T(u),v> = <u,T*(v)> for all u,v ∈ V. In matrix form, conjugate transpose.
An operator T where T* = T. All eigenvalues are real, and eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.
For subspace W, the orthogonal projection of v onto W is the unique vector pw ∈ W such that v - pw is orthogonal to W.

front: What is the best approximation theorem?
back: If W is a finite-dimensional subspace, the orthogonal projection of v onto W is the closest point in W to v.
An operator T such that TT* = T*T. T is normal iff there exists an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.
An operator T preserving inner product: <T(u),T(v)> = <u,v>. In real spaces called orthogonal, in complex called unitary.
